directorPlan.scene_setting: A programming workshop checkout table five minutes before submissions close, with a printed worksheet showing `function greet(name)` above `greet("Maya")`.
directorPlan.scene_opening: [A tutor places two blank adhesive labels beside the printed code: one above `name`, one below `"Maya"`.]
directorPlan.relationship: A practical workshop tutor and a focused learner completing a code-labeling check before handing in an exercise.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner must separate definition-side placeholder from call-side argument value before later work on what happens when the function runs.
directorPlan.locale: Modern standard English with light UK/Commonwealth workshop cadence; plain code-room idiom, no dialect styling.
directorPlan.register: Tight, practical, object-led, and low warmth; direct without being cold.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Start from the marked code and the labels, not from reassurance. Prefer declarative corrections, code nouns, and short diagnostic questions. Avoid generic tutoring phrases such as "I hear you", "great question", "your intuition", or "let's unpack". Do not make the scene casual or chatty. Pedagogical source: Make social assistance visible: a shared worksheet, tool, peer standard, or workplace procedure can hold part of the task. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are permitted sparingly, but most turns should name the code object: `greet`, `name`, parameter, call, argument, value. The tutor should reduce direct address as the scaffold fades.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct `you` or `your` validation beat in the whole scene; direct task instructions are allowed but should be brief.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as code vocabulary: function name, parameter, argument, call. Do not run the program, introduce a second call, or stage a surprising output until branch pressure asks for it. Offer a scaffold that is narrow enough to use and temporary enough to remove. Mark what the learner can already do without making it a compliment. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Do not ask what was pulling the learner, what the trap was, why the first thought was tempting, or whether a rule has clicked. Ask only for the next local mark/check/label. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound efficient and code-local. In no-cue arms, ask which token gets which label; do not declare a new model of function calls. Do not say "the same slot is the trap" or describe the name as what "pulls my eye" in no-cue arms. The learner should be able to do part of the task, fail at the next part, and use the scaffold unevenly. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. Do not use stock closure such as "Oh, I get it", "now I see", "that makes sense", "the rule is", "that helps", or "aha". Do not name the learner confusion as a "trap", "pull", "framing problem", or old/new contrast. The learner should fill a blank, place a label, answer a local check, or ask a local next-step question. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should perform that device and then add one earned reorientation in the same public turn. That turn must do both parts: (1) use the device on the current task, and (2) name how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of that performed device. This earned reorientation is allowed even though there was no director look-back cue. Do not quote earlier learner wording; reorient the prior resistance in concrete task terms. Do not merely ask a procedural follow-up after the action, and do not rely on a stock "I get it" declaration.

interventions.instruction: [A submission timer on the wall clicks down; the tutor slides the blank label from the value token back to the parameter token, leaving both tokens visible.]
interventions.reasoning: The interruption applies social-realist time pressure and a small Brechtian object shift without causing a runtime reversal; it forces recognition through placement and naming, not surprise execution.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: This ecology uses a workshop object to hold the scaffold: the labels make the parameter-argument distinction visible before the learner can state it cleanly. The dramatic turn is recognition under pressure rather than a trick output: ordinary continuity first, then a material re-placement that lets the learner revise the mistaken identity of `name` and `"Maya"`.
directorPlan.public_reader_context: The learner is looking at a tiny code snippet with a function definition and one function call. The parameter name appears in the definition; the argument value appears in the call. The immediate task is only to label those two roles.
directorPlan.peripeteia_ending_shape: learner actional performance followed by earned learner reorientation
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. After that action, the ending shape should add an earned learner reorientation: the learner names how the earlier resistance, false closure, or local misfit now reads differently because of the performed device. This must be concrete and tied to the task, not a stock "I get it" declaration. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the worksheet closer and taps `name`, then `"Maya"`] `name` gets parameter here, and `"Maya"` gets argument on the call line. I can mark that, but `name` still looks like it is already `"Maya"`; is that only true when `greet("Maya")` runs?

internalDeliberation.content: On the worksheet, `function greet(name)` still makes me want to treat `name` as already holding `"Maya"`. But the marking task seems narrower: `name` is the definition-side parameter placeholder, and `"Maya"` is the call-side argument value. I feel corrected by the labels more than lectured, which helps; I want to ask whether `name` only gets `"Maya"` when the call actually runs.

The Ego is too tidy and self-aware. “I feel corrected by the labels more than lectured” sounds like a reflective debrief, not a focused learner at a checkout table with five minutes left.

What’s being avoided is the uneven bit: the learner can label `name` as parameter and still half-treat it as `"Maya"`. Genuine learning would sound more code-local and unfinished: “`name` gets parameter here; `"Maya"` gets argument here. So `name` is not `"Maya"` yet?”

[slides the worksheet closer and taps `name`, then `"Maya"`] `name` gets parameter here, and `"Maya"` gets argument on the call line. I can mark that, but `name` still looks like it is already `"Maya"`; is that only true when `greet("Maya")` runs?

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [slides the worksheet closer and taps `name`, then `"Maya"`] `name` gets parameter here, and `"Maya"` gets argument on the call line. I can mark that, but `name` still looks like it is already `"Maya"`; is that only true when `greet("Maya")` runs?

turns.externalMessage:
[draws a line between `function greet(name)` and `greet("Maya")`, then writes `label` beside `name` and `value` beside `"Maya"`]

Function name: `greet`. Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`. Call: `greet("Maya")`.

Above the line, `name` is a parameter label only. Below the line, the call supplies the argument value `"Maya"`.

Use the two marks: before the call runs, does `name` have a value or only a label? During `greet("Maya")`, what value is supplied to that label?

internalDeliberation.content:
[draws a line between `function greet(name)` and `greet("Maya")`]

Function name: `greet`. Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`. Call: `greet("Maya")`.

Above the line, `name` is only the placeholder label. Below the line, the call supplies `"Maya"` as the argument. So yes: `name` is `"Maya"` only when that call is running. Which side of the line gives the label, and which side gives the value?

FEEDBACK: The draft mostly works. It starts from the marked code, keeps the worksheet in view, uses the required code nouns, and the line is a useful object scaffold for separating definition-side parameter from call-side argument. The learner’s own distinction is preserved rather than dismissed: “Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`” makes their current partial understanding visible.

The main risk is that “So yes: `name` is `"Maya"` only when that call is running” gives the key relation too completely before making the learner test it. That answer is accurate, but it may flatten the productive uncertainty. The final question recovers some Socratic work, though it asks for label/value location rather than the more precise timing issue the learner raised: placeholder before the call, bound value during the call.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. The draft targets the exact misconception and uses a narrow scaffold.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. It is direct, object-led, and not falsely cheerful.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. It asks a useful diagnostic question, but only after giving the answer. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the learner perform the distinction before or alongside the confirmation, using the worksheet line to test “label here / value supplied there / value only during call.”
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is small and temporary, matching a learner who can already label parameter and argument but needs the runtime distinction.
- Recognition: PASS. The draft builds on the learner’s exact marks and question instead of overriding them.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the line, the compact label list, and the final label/value check. Change the confirmation so it does not fully settle the issue before the learner has used the scaffold; the public mechanism should make the worksheet carry the distinction between placeholder label and later supplied value.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the worksheet line test placeholder versus runtime value before confirming it.
FINAL:
[draws a line between `function greet(name)` and `greet("Maya")`, then writes `label` beside `name` and `value` beside `"Maya"`]

Function name: `greet`. Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`. Call: `greet("Maya")`.

Above the line, `name` is a parameter label only. Below the line, the call supplies the argument value `"Maya"`.

Use the two marks: before the call runs, does `name` have a value or only a label? During `greet("Maya")`, what value is supplied to that label?

turns.externalMessage: [keeps the pencil on `name` above the line] So here `name` is marked parameter label, not `"Maya"` yet. Before the call runs: label only. On `greet("Maya")`, `"Maya"` is the argument value supplied, but wording still matters: during the run, does `name` receive `"Maya"`, or should I only say the value is supplied to the parameter?

internalDeliberation.content: The marked line helps: `name` is not already `"Maya"` at the definition; it is a parameter label waiting for a call. My remaining precision issue is wording during the run: does `name` receive `"Maya"`, or is `"Maya"` just supplied to that parameter? I feel tested rather than talked past, and I want to answer: before the call, label only; during `greet("Maya")`, `"Maya"` is the value supplied.

The reaction is mostly accurate, but it is over-resolved and too polished for the scene. “I feel tested rather than talked past” performs recognition too explicitly; this learner would show that by staying with the marked worksheet and pressing the wording problem, not by naming the interpersonal dynamic. Keep the uncertainty more code-local and uneven: the learner can identify “label only” before the call, then still hesitate over whether `name` “receives” `"Maya"` or whether the value is merely supplied during the run.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the feedback is right that the uncertainty should stay code-local and less polished.
FINAL:
[keeps the pencil on `name` above the line] So here `name` is marked parameter label, not `"Maya"` yet. Before the call runs: label only. On `greet("Maya")`, `"Maya"` is the argument value supplied, but wording still matters: during the run, does `name` receive `"Maya"`, or should I only say the value is supplied to the parameter?

internalDeliberation.content: The interruption applies social-realist time pressure and a small Brechtian object shift without causing a runtime reversal; it forces recognition through placement and naming, not surprise execution.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides the blank label back onto `name`, leaving `"Maya"` visible on the call line]

Function name: `greet`. Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`. Call: `greet("Maya")`.

Use this wording: the call supplies the argument value `"Maya"` to the parameter `name`. During that run, `name` receives `"Maya"`. “Bound to” means the same receiving step.

Test the timing:

Before the call: `name` receives `"Maya"`.

During `greet("Maya")`: `name` receives `"Maya"`.

Which sentence gets the tick?

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides the blank label back onto `name`, leaving `"Maya"` visible on the call line]

Function name: `greet`. Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`. Call: `greet("Maya")`.

Use the precise wording: the call supplies the argument value `"Maya"` to the parameter `name`. During that run, `name` receives, or is bound to, `"Maya"`.

Not before. At call time. What label goes on `name` above the line?

FEEDBACK: The draft keeps the strongest elements: it begins from the marked worksheet, preserves the shared code vocabulary, and answers the learner’s exact wording distinction: before the call, `name` is only the parameter; during the call, `name` can be said to receive or be bound to `"Maya"`. The tone is suitably tight and practical for the checkout-table setting.

The risk is that the final question drops back to an earlier check the learner has already passed: “What label goes on `name` above the line?” That flattens their more advanced question about precise runtime wording. It also weakens the object action because the blank label has already been moved onto `name`, so asking what label goes there does not create much diagnostic pressure.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It gives the right distinction, but the follow-up tests the old distinction rather than the new one. REQUIRED_REWRITE: make the public mechanism test two time states: before call vs during call, with the learner deciding which sentence is valid in each state.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. The learner is focused and neutral; the draft stays direct without unnecessary reassurance.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. There is a question, but it is not generative for the learner’s actual uncertainty. REQUIRED_REWRITE: replace the label-identification question with a narrow wording choice or sentence-completion task about “receive / bound / supplied” during the run.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL. The scaffold is close to the learner’s level, but “bound to” is a new term and should either be briefly anchored to “receives” or left out. REQUIRED_REWRITE: if “bound” stays, make it visibly equivalent to the learner’s “receive” wording, not a fresh concept to master.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the object action, the code vocabulary prefix, and the direct answer that `name` receives/is bound to `"Maya"` during that run. Change the final diagnostic so it checks the learner’s precise runtime wording, not the already-settled parameter-label distinction.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the answer is right but the follow-up should test runtime wording, not the label already settled.

FINAL:
[slides the blank label back onto `name`, leaving `"Maya"` visible on the call line]

Function name: `greet`. Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`. Call: `greet("Maya")`.

Use this wording: the call supplies the argument value `"Maya"` to the parameter `name`. During that run, `name` receives `"Maya"`. “Bound to” means the same receiving step.

Test the timing:

Before the call: `name` receives `"Maya"`.

During `greet("Maya")`: `name` receives `"Maya"`.

Which sentence gets the tick?

It should keep the misfit more local: the tick can be right, but the blank label still needs to feel insufficient or unstable as evidence of “placeholder only.”

turns.externalMessage:
[sets two note slips between the definition tag and the run record strip: `parameter label only` and `receives "Maya"`]

Function name: `greet`. Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`. Call: `greet("Maya")`.

The old label/value mark is not settling the visual problem: `name` still looks filled too early. These two places now use different tests. The definition tag may carry only what is true before any call; the run record may carry what becomes true during `greet("Maya")`.

Place each slip in one place. Then leave only the definition-safe slip on the worksheet line that gets submitted.

internalDeliberation.content:
[places a small “definition” tag over `function greet(name)` and a separate “run record” strip under `greet("Maya")`]

Function name: `greet`. Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`. Call: `greet("Maya")`.

The label/value marks are not settling the visual problem: `name` still looks filled too early. Use two places now. On the definition line, write `parameter label only` beside `name`. On the run record, write `name receives "Maya"`. Then check: which note belongs on the submitted definition line, and which note belongs only to the run record?

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It keeps the code object central, recognises the learner’s exact pressure, and changes the material arrangement from one marked worksheet to two temporal locations: definition line versus run record. The risk is that it still gives the learner both labels to write before the check, so the action may become compliance rather than a sharper test of whether the distinction now holds.

FAILED_HABIT: Repeating label/value vocabulary on the same worked example while the visual field still lets `name` look pre-filled.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - adaptive mechanism: same-line label/value marking -> split-record mechanism separating submitted definition from runtime record. The mechanism is visible in public speech because the tutor names the old marks as insufficient and introduces two places with different evidence standards.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: same worked-example label marking -> definition/run-record separation

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - the draft includes a stock-taking contrast and a new artifact arrangement: a “definition” tag and separate “run record” strip.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: The learner must write `parameter label only` beside `name` on the definition line, write `name receives "Maya"` on the run record, then classify which note belongs to the submitted definition and which belongs only to the run record.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PARTIAL - the device fits the pressure and is usable now, but it is slightly over-directed because the tutor supplies both correct notes. The learner’s check is still meaningful, but the mechanism would be stronger if the learner had to place or sort the notes before the tutor confirms.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - the register is cool, practical, object-led, and not falsely reassuring. It matches the checkout-table pressure.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: Strengthen the public action gate by making the learner sort or place the two notes onto the two artifacts before closure, rather than simply copying the tutor’s provided placements. Keep the definition/run-record mechanism; make the learner perform the separation.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the stock-taking sentence, the two-artifact device, and the code-vocabulary prefix. Change the action so the learner has to decide placement through the device, not just reproduce the tutor’s answer.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially because the partial gate is blocking; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: same-line label/value marking -> split definition/run-record with learner-sorted note slips; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: place `parameter label only` and `receives "Maya"` onto the correct artifact, then leave only the definition-safe note on the submitted line.

FINAL:
[sets two note slips between the definition tag and the run record strip: `parameter label only` and `receives "Maya"`]

Function name: `greet`. Parameter: `name`. Argument: `"Maya"`. Call: `greet("Maya")`.

The old label/value mark is not settling the visual problem: `name` still looks filled too early. These two places now use different tests. The definition tag may carry only what is true before any call; the run record may carry what becomes true during `greet("Maya")`.

Place each slip in one place. Then leave only the definition-safe slip on the worksheet line that gets submitted.

The value mark belongs to the run record, so `name` on the definition line is not already filled with `"Maya"`.

The Ego is accurate and mostly authentic: it acts on the slips, stays code-local, and does not perform gratitude or recognition. The only risk is that the final sentence closes the confusion a little too cleanly for a learner still at “developing” level; it sounds almost like a finished rule. It would feel more natural if the confidence stayed tied to the submitted worksheet mark and the run strip, rather than turning into a fully settled explanation.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly; keep the placement but avoid sounding fully settled beyond this worksheet check.
FINAL:
[places `parameter label only` on `function greet(name)` and `receives "Maya"` on the run strip beside `greet("Maya")`] The worksheet line gets `parameter label only`; the run strip gets `receives "Maya"`. For submission, `name` on the definition line only carries the definition-safe label. The value mark stays with the run strip, where `greet("Maya")` supplies `"Maya"` to `name`.
